title: User-defined fn — grammar, parser, compiler
analysis:
This task adds user-defined function declarations across three layers: grammar, parser, and compiler, with overload resolution for function calls.

**Existing infrastructure:**
- Grammar has `_declaration` choice with `structure_definition`, `import_declaration`, `enum_declaration`. Functions become a 4th top-level declaration.
- Grammar has `let_declaration` as a structure member (no semicolons). Function bodies need a separate `fn_let_binding` rule with `;` separator to distinguish let bindings from the final result expression.
- Grammar has `type_expr: $ => $.identifier` — simple identifier-based types, sufficient for fn param/return types.
- Grammar has `function_call` expression rule with `argument_list` — already parses `name(args)`. Compiler currently emits `CompiledExprKind::FunctionCall` for stdlib calls; user fn calls need a new `CompiledExprKind::UserFunctionCall`.
- Parser uses two-pass lowering: first pass collects enum names, second pass lowers everything. Functions will be collected in the first pass for forward reference support.
- Compiler has `CompilationScope` for name resolution within structures. Function compilation needs its own scope with params + let bindings.
- `CompiledExprKind` in reify-types/src/expr.rs needs a new `UserFunctionCall` variant; reify-expr match arms on that enum need updating.
- Tests follow established pattern: parser tests in ts_parser.rs inline #[cfg(test)] mod, compiler tests in boundary2_producer.rs using parse-compile-assert pattern.

**Key design:** Function bodies have `let_declaration`s terminated by `;` followed by a final result expression. Type parameters (`<T: Bound>`) are parsed structurally but not deeply type-checked in this task. Overload resolution matches compiled argument types against function parameter types. Exactly one candidate must match or an error is emitted.

prerequisites.description:
Add grammar rules to tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js:
1. Add `function_definition` to `_declaration` choice.
2. Define rules: `function_definition` (optional 'pub', 'fn', name, optional type_parameters, '(' params ')', optional return type, fn_body), `fn_param_list` (comma-separated fn_param with optional trailing comma), `fn_param` (name ':' type_expr), `fn_body` ('{' repeat(fn_let_binding) result_expression '}'), `fn_let_binding` (like let_declaration but with ';' terminator, no where clause), `type_parameters` ('<' comma-sep type_param_bound '>'), `type_param_bound` (identifier optional(':' trait_bound_list)), `trait_bound_list` (identifier repeat('+' identifier)).
3. Add `[$.fn_param]` and `[$.fn_let_binding]` to conflicts array.
4. Run `tree-sitter generate` to regenerate parser.c, grammar.json, node-types.json.
Note: Use a separate `fn_let_binding` rule (not the existing `let_declaration`) because fn body lets end with ';' and have no where clause.

Add parser-level AST types to crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs:
(a) Add `Declaration::Function(FnDef)` variant to Declaration enum.
(b) Add `FnDef` struct: `name: String, is_pub: bool, type_params: Vec<TypeParamDecl>, params: Vec<FnParam>, return_type: Option<TypeExpr>, body: FnBody, span: SourceSpan, content_hash: ContentHash` (derive Debug, Clone).
(c) Add `TypeParamDecl` struct: `name: String, bounds: Vec<String>` (derive Debug, Clone).
(d) Add `FnParam` struct: `name: String, type_expr: TypeExpr, span: SourceSpan` (derive Debug, Clone).
(e) Add `FnBody` struct: `let_bindings: Vec<LetDecl>, result_expr: Expr` (derive Debug, Clone). Reuses existing LetDecl for the body's let bindings.
These are type-only changes with no logic.

Add `CompiledExprKind::UserFunctionCall { function_name: String, args: Vec<CompiledExpr> }` variant to the CompiledExprKind enum in crates/reify-types/src/expr.rs. Also add compiled function types to crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:
(a) `CompiledFunction { name: String, is_pub: bool, params: Vec<(String, Type)>, return_type: Type, body: CompiledFnBody, content_hash: ContentHash }` (derive Debug, Clone).
(b) `CompiledFnBody { let_bindings: Vec<(String, CompiledExpr)>, result_expr: CompiledExpr }` (derive Debug, Clone).
(c) Add `pub functions: Vec<CompiledFunction>` field to `CompiledModule`.
These are type-only additions — compilation and initialization will come in TDD steps.

Add match arm for `CompiledExprKind::UserFunctionCall` in crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs eval_expr function. For now, return `Value::Undef` (eval of user functions is a separate task). This prevents the exhaustive match from failing after adding the new variant. Also update the `assert_no_unresolved` helper in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs to handle the new variant: recurse into args.

steps.description: Write failing test in crates/reify-syntax/src/ts_parser.rs: `parse_simple_function_definition` — parse source `"fn area(w: Scalar, h: Scalar) -> Scalar { w * h }"`, assert: no parse errors, module.declarations has 1 entry, it is `Declaration::Function(f)` with f.name == "area", f.is_pub == false, f.params.len() == 2, f.params[0].name == "w", f.params[0].type_expr.name == "Scalar", f.params[1].name == "h", f.return_type.is_some() with name "Scalar", f.body.let_bindings.is_empty(), f.body.result_expr is BinOp(*). Fails because lower_source_file doesn't handle function_definition yet.

Add CST→AST lowering for function_definition in crates/reify-syntax/src/ts_parser.rs:
(a) In lower_source_file second pass, add `"function_definition"` match arm that calls self.lower_function(child) and pushes Declaration::Function(...).
(b) Implement lower_function(): check for 'pub' keyword in children, extract name field, extract optional type_parameters (lower_type_parameters helper), collect fn_param children from fn_param_list, extract optional return type after '->', lower fn_body (collect fn_let_binding children as LetDecl, lower final expression as result_expr). Return FnDef.
(c) Implement lower_fn_param(): extract name field and type field.
(d) Implement lower_fn_body(): iterate children, collect fn_let_binding nodes (lower name/value, create LetDecl without where_clause), find the trailing expression node and lower it as result_expr.
(e) Implement lower_type_parameters(): iterate type_param_bound children, extract name and optional bounds.
Step-1 test now passes.

Write test in crates/reify-syntax/src/ts_parser.rs: `parse_pub_function_with_conditional` — parse source `"pub fn clamp(x: Real, lo: Real, hi: Real) -> Real { if x < lo then lo else if x > hi then hi else x }"`, assert: no parse errors, Declaration::Function(f) with f.is_pub == true, f.params.len() == 3 (x, lo, hi all with type Real), f.return_type name == "Real", f.body.result_expr is ExprKind::Conditional. Should pass with step-2 implementation.

Verify step-3 test passes with step-2 implementation. If it fails, fix any issues with pub keyword detection or conditional expression lowering within fn_body. The existing lower_expr already handles conditional_expression.

Write test in crates/reify-syntax/src/ts_parser.rs: `parse_function_with_let_bindings` — parse source `"fn f(x: Real) -> Real { let y = x * 2; y + 1 }"`, assert: no parse errors, Declaration::Function(f) with f.params.len() == 1, f.body.let_bindings.len() == 1, f.body.let_bindings[0].name == "y", f.body.let_bindings[0].value is BinOp(*), f.body.result_expr is BinOp(+). Should pass with step-2 implementation.

Verify step-5 test passes with step-2 implementation. If it fails, fix fn_body lowering to correctly separate fn_let_binding nodes (which end with ';') from the final result expression. The key is that fn_let_binding is a named node kind distinct from the result expression.

Write test in crates/reify-syntax/src/ts_parser.rs: `parse_function_with_type_parameters` — parse source `"fn identity<T>(x: T) -> T { x }"`, assert: no parse errors, Declaration::Function(f) with f.type_params.len() == 1, f.type_params[0].name == "T", f.type_params[0].bounds.is_empty(). Also test with bounds: parse `"fn add<T: Numeric>(a: T, b: T) -> T { a + b }"`, assert f.type_params[0].bounds == ["Numeric"]. Should pass with step-2 implementation.

Verify step-7 test passes. If it fails, fix lower_type_parameters() to correctly extract type param names and bounds from the CST. Type parameters are optional, so the simple function tests (steps 1-6) should still pass.

Write failing test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_simple_function` — parse source `"fn double(x: Real) -> Real { x + x }"`, compile, assert: compiled.functions.len() == 1, compiled.functions[0].name == "double", compiled.functions[0].is_pub == false, compiled.functions[0].params == [("x", Type::Real)], compiled.functions[0].return_type == Type::Real, compiled.functions[0].body.let_bindings.is_empty(), compiled.functions[0].body.result_expr.result_type == Type::Real. No error diagnostics. Fails because compile() doesn't process Declaration::Function yet.

Add function compilation to crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:
(a) In compile(), initialize `let mut functions = Vec::new();` and add match arm for `Declaration::Function(fn_def)`: call compile_function() and push result.
(b) Include functions in CompiledModule construction and content_hash computation.
(c) Implement compile_function(): resolve param types via resolve_type_name, resolve return type, create a CompilationScope with function params registered, compile each body let binding (adding each to scope), compile result_expr, build CompiledFunction.
(d) compile_function returns CompiledFunction with content_hash computed from name + params + body.
Step-9 test now passes.

Write test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_function_with_let_bindings` — parse source `"fn f(x: Real) -> Real { let y = x + x; let z = y * y; z + 1 }"`, compile, assert: compiled.functions[0].body.let_bindings.len() == 2, let_bindings[0].0 == "y", let_bindings[1].0 == "z", result_expr references z (is BinOp with ValueRef or at least compiles without 'unresolved name' errors). No error diagnostics. Should pass with step-10 implementation.

Verify step-11 test passes. Ensure that compile_function adds each let binding to the scope progressively so later bindings and the result expression can reference earlier bindings. If there are 'unresolved name' diagnostics for y or z, fix the scope registration order in compile_function.

Write test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_overloaded_functions` — parse source with two functions sharing the same name but different param types: `"fn convert(x: Real) -> Real { x }\nfn convert(x: Int) -> Int { x }"`, compile, assert: compiled.functions.len() == 2, both have name "convert", first has params [("x", Type::Real)], second has params [("x", Type::Int)], no error diagnostics.

Verify step-13 test passes. The current compile_function + push approach naturally allows multiple functions with the same name. If duplicates are being rejected, adjust. No additional logic needed for allowing overloads — just push all compiled functions without deduplication.

Write failing test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_duplicate_function_signature_error` — parse source with two functions having identical name AND param types: `"fn f(x: Real) -> Real { x }\nfn f(x: Real) -> Int { x }"`, compile, assert: diagnostics is non-empty, at least one diagnostic mentions duplicate or conflict. Fails because compile() doesn't check for duplicate signatures yet.

Add duplicate function signature detection in crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs compile():
After compiling all functions, or during registration, maintain a HashMap<(String, Vec<Type>), usize> tracking (name, param_types) → index. Before inserting, check if the key already exists. If so, emit a diagnostic: 'duplicate function signature: fn_name(param_types)'. Still register the function (for error recovery) but flag the conflict.
Step-15 test now passes.

Write failing test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_user_function_call` — parse source `"fn double(x: Real) -> Real { x + x }\nstructure S { let v = double(3) }"`, compile, assert: the 'v' value cell's default_expr.kind is `CompiledExprKind::UserFunctionCall { function_name: "double", args }` with args.len() == 1, and result_type == Type::Real. No error diagnostics. Fails because compile_expr currently emits stdlib FunctionCall for all function calls.

Add overload resolution to compile_expr's FunctionCall arm in crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:
(a) Thread compiled functions list (or a function registry) through compile_structure → compile_expr. Add `functions: &[CompiledFunction]` parameter to compile_expr (or pass via a new CompilationContext struct).
(b) In the FunctionCall match arm, after compiling args: first check user-defined functions for a match (name matches AND arg count matches AND each compiled_arg.result_type matches the function param type). If exactly one user fn matches, emit `CompiledExprKind::UserFunctionCall { function_name, args }` with result_type from the matching function's return_type and an appropriate content_hash.
(c) If no user fn matches, fall through to existing stdlib FunctionCall logic.
(d) If multiple user fns match (ambiguous overload), emit a diagnostic.
Step-17 test now passes.

Write test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_overload_resolution_picks_correct` — parse source with two overloaded user fns and a call that matches one: `"fn process(x: Real) -> Real { x + 1 }\nfn process(x: Int) -> Int { x }\nstructure S { let a = process(3.14) }"`, compile, assert: 'a' value cell expr is UserFunctionCall with function_name "process", result_type == Type::Real (matched the Real overload since 3.14 is Real). No error diagnostics.

Verify step-19 test passes with step-18 implementation. The overload resolution should match based on arg types: 3.14 compiles to Real, so it matches `process(x: Real)`. If it fails, check the arg type inference (3.14 should be Real not Int) and the type matching logic.

Write test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `compile_function_forward_reference` — parse source where a structure calls a function declared AFTER it: `"structure S { let v = double(3) }\nfn double(x: Real) -> Real { x + x }"`, compile, assert: 'v' value cell expr is UserFunctionCall, no error diagnostics. This validates order-independent declarations. May fail if functions aren't collected in a pre-pass.

Ensure forward references work by using a two-pass approach in compile():
(a) Pre-pass: compile ALL function declarations first (before processing structures), so the full function registry is available when compiling structure members.
(b) This mirrors the existing two-pass pattern for enum_defs.
If step-21 already passes because compile() processes all declarations in order and functions are compiled before structures in step-10's implementation, no changes needed. Otherwise, reorder to pre-compile functions.
Step-21 test now passes.

Write E2E regression test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: `e2e_function_with_structure_unchanged` — parse the existing bracket_source(), compile, verify all existing assertions still hold: 6 value cells, 3 constraints, 1 realization, no error diagnostics. Also verify compiled.functions.is_empty() (bracket has no fn declarations). This confirms backward compatibility.
Verify step-23 passes. Run full `cargo test --workspace` to confirm no regressions across all crates. Fix any issues found.

design_decisions.decision: Separate `fn_let_binding` grammar rule instead of reusing `let_declaration`
design_decisions.rationale: Structure-level `let_declaration` has no semicolons and supports optional `where_clause`. Function body let bindings need `;` terminators to separate them from the final result expression, and don't support where clauses. A separate `fn_let_binding` rule avoids grammar conflicts and keeps the two contexts clean.

Per task spec: 'distinct from stdlib FunctionCall so eval can dispatch correctly'. User function calls need different eval semantics (body evaluation with argument substitution) vs stdlib calls (dispatch to reify_stdlib::eval_builtin). Keeping them as separate variants makes the dispatch clear and avoids runtime checks.

design_decisions.decision: Type parameters parsed structurally but not deeply type-checked
design_decisions.rationale: The task specifies grammar and parser support for type parameters (`<T: Bound>`), but the test cases don't exercise generic type checking. Full generic/trait checking is a deeper M5 feature. We parse the type params into TypeParamDecl structs for future use, but the compiler ignores them for now beyond storing them in CompiledFunction.

design_decisions.decision: Function compilation happens in a pre-pass before structure compilation
design_decisions.rationale: Order-independent declarations require that a structure can call a function declared after it. Following the established two-pass pattern used for enum_defs, functions are compiled in a pre-pass so the full function registry is available when compiling structure members.

design_decisions.decision: Overload resolution matches compiled argument types against function parameter types
design_decisions.rationale: Since compile_expr already produces CompiledExpr with result_type, we can compare compiled arg types against function param types at compile time. This catches type mismatches early and selects the correct overload. Exactly one candidate must match — zero matches falls through to stdlib, multiple matches is an error.

design_decisions.decision: Function body let bindings reuse the existing LetDecl AST type
design_decisions.rationale: The LetDecl struct already has name, type_expr, value, span, content_hash — everything needed for fn body let bindings. The only difference is fn body lets don't have where_clause (set to None). Reusing LetDecl avoids duplicating AST types and leverages existing lowering infrastructure.

design_decisions.decision: Thread function registry through compile_expr via additional parameter
design_decisions.rationale: Adding `functions: &[CompiledFunction]` to compile_expr's parameter list follows the existing pattern of threading `enum_defs: &[reify_types::EnumDef]`. This keeps the compiler's functional style and avoids introducing mutable shared state.

design_decisions.decision: Duplicate detection uses (name, param_types) as key, not (name, param_types, return_type)
design_decisions.rationale: Per task spec: 'Same name + same param types = error'. Return type alone doesn't distinguish overloads — you can't have two functions with the same name and param types but different return types. This matches standard overload resolution rules (C++, Java, etc.).

reuse.how: Extended to collect function names in the first pass (alongside enum names) for forward reference support. Second pass lowers function_definition nodes using the same dispatch pattern as structure/enum/import.
Extended to pre-compile all functions before processing structures, ensuring the full function registry is available for overload resolution. Same pattern as enum_defs collection.

Reused to create a function-local scope with params registered, then let bindings added progressively. compile_function creates a new CompilationScope(fn_name) and uses scope.register() for each param and let binding.
Reused as-is to compile function body expressions (both let binding values and result expression). Extended with an additional functions parameter for overload resolution in the FunctionCall arm.
Reused to resolve fn param types and return types from TypeExpr names to Type values.
Reused in lower_function(), lower_fn_param(), lower_fn_body() for extracting text, building spans, and computing content hashes.
Reused for fn body let bindings. FnBody.let_bindings is Vec<LetDecl> with where_clause set to None.
Parse source string → compile → assert on compiled.functions/diagnostics/templates. Same helper patterns (parse, assert no errors, extract fields).
Used to build content hashes for CompiledFunction from name + param types + body expression hashes, following the same pattern as TopologyTemplate hash computation.
Same diagnostic pattern reused for duplicate function signature errors, unresolved type errors in fn params, and ambiguous overload resolution errors.
